questions.question: What is the advantage of @ConfigurationProperties over @Value for a group of related settings?
options: @ConfigurationProperties supports arrays; @Value does not
@ConfigurationProperties maps a group of properties to a typed class with IDE support, validation, and no string keys scattered in code
@Value requires a restart to pick up changes; @ConfigurationProperties reloads live
@ConfigurationProperties is faster at startup because it reads all properties in one pass

questions.question: In application.properties, what does ${STRIPE_KEY:defaultValue} mean?
options: The property is mandatory — throw an error if STRIPE_KEY is not set
Use the environment variable STRIPE_KEY if set; otherwise fall back to 'defaultValue'
Read STRIPE_KEY from a YAML file named defaultValue
Encrypt the value STRIPE_KEY using the key defaultValue

questions.question: What is the highest-priority property source in Spring Boot's override hierarchy?
options: application.properties
application-{profile}.properties
OS environment variables
Command-line arguments (--key=value)

questions.question: Adding @Validated to a @ConfigurationProperties class does what?
options: Validates the properties at request time when they're first injected
Validates the property values at application startup — fails fast if config is invalid
Enables runtime validation only in test environments
Generates getter validation in the compiled code

questions.question: Which approach is safer for storing a production API key?
options: app.api.key=sk_live_abc123 in application.properties committed to git
app.api.key=${API_KEY} in application.properties, with API_KEY set as an environment variable
API key hardcoded as a private static final String in the service class
API key stored in a @Bean method inside a @Configuration class
